title: Brüderchen und Schwesterchen
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 11. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The eleventh tale in the Grimms' collection (KHM 11), the story of a brother and sister who flee a witch-stepmother into the forest, where three enchanted brooks warn that whoever drinks will become a beast; at the third the boy, unable to bear his thirst, drinks and is turned into a roe. His faithful sister tends him; a king marries her; and when the witch murders the young queen and slips her own one-eyed daughter into the bed in her place, the dead queen returns three nights running to nurse her child and stroke the roe, until the king holds her fast and she is restored to life. Nothing is softened: the false bride is torn apart by wild beasts and the witch burned to ashes, and only then does the spell break and the brother regain his human shape. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as todt, roth, gieng and aufgieng, daß, muß and wüßte, Thier and Thür, thun/that, tödten and geknieet.

sentences.t: Brüderchen nahm sein Schwesterchen an der Hand und sprach „seit die Mutter todt ist, haben wir keine gute Stunde mehr; die Stiefmutter schlägt uns alle Tage, und wenn wir zu ihr kommen, stößt sie uns mit den Füßen fort.
sentences.l: Little-brother took his little-sister by the hand and spoke, “since the mother is dead, we have no good hour anymore; the stepmother beats us all day, and when we come to her, she thrusts us away with her feet.
sentences.i: Little brother took his little sister by the hand and said, “Since Mother died, we have not had one good hour; the stepmother beats us every day, and when we come to her, she kicks us away.
sentences.n: “todt” is the 1857 spelling of tot (dead), keeping the old -dt; the edition preserves many such archaic spellings you will meet throughout (roth, tödten, Thier, Thür). “Brüderchen” and “Schwesterchen” are the -chen diminutives that name the pair throughout — literally ‘little brother, little sister.’ “stößt … fort” is the separable verb fortstoßen, ‘to thrust away.’

sentences.t: Die harten Brotkrusten, die übrig bleiben, sind unsere Speise, und dem Hündlein unter dem Tisch gehts besser: dem wirft sie doch manchmal einen guten Bissen zu.
sentences.l: The hard bread-crusts that are left over are our food, and the little-dog under the table has it better: to it she does throw a good morsel sometimes.
sentences.i: The hard bread-crusts that are left over are our food, and the little dog under the table is better off: to him she does throw a good morsel now and then.
sentences.n: “gehts” = geht es (‘it goes,’ i.e. fares); “dem Hündlein … gehts besser” is the impersonal dative idiom jemandem geht es gut/besser, ‘someone fares (better).’ “wirft … zu” is the separable verb zuwerfen (‘to throw to’), the prefix zu jumping to the end. The fronted “dem” resumes the little dog for emphasis.

sentences.t: Daß Gott erbarm, wenn das unsere Mutter wüßte!
sentences.l: That God have mercy, if our mother knew this!
sentences.i: God have mercy — if our mother knew of this!
sentences.n: “Daß Gott erbarm” is a fixed exclamation of pity, ‘God have mercy’ (erbarm is a shortened old subjunctive of erbarmen). “wüßte” is subjunctive II of wissen (‘knew’), the 1857 spelling with ß — the hypothetical ‘if only she knew.’

sentences.t: Komm, wir wollen miteinander in die weite Welt gehen.“
sentences.l: Come, we will go together out into the wide world.”
sentences.i: Come, let us go out together into the wide world.”
sentences.n: “wir wollen … gehen” — wollen here is the hortative ‘let us,’ not ‘want.’ “in die weite Welt gehen” is the stock fairy-tale phrase for setting out into the world.

sentences.t: Sie giengen den ganzen Tag über Wiesen, Felder und Steine, und wenn es regnete, sprach das Schwesterchen „Gott und unsere Herzen die weinen zusammen!“
sentences.l: They went the whole day over meadows, fields and stones, and when it rained, the little-sister spoke, “God and our hearts, they weep together!”
sentences.i: They walked the whole day over meadows, fields and stones, and whenever it rained, the little sister said, “God and our hearts weep together!”
sentences.n: “giengen” is the 1857 spelling of gingen (‘went’); this -ie- spelling of gehen recurs (gieng, aufgieng). The fronted “die” in “Gott und unsere Herzen die weinen” resumes the subjects before the verb — a folk-poetic rhythm.

sentences.t: Abends kamen sie in einen großen Wald und waren so müde von Jammer, Hunger und dem langen Weg, daß sie sich in einen hohlen Baum setzten und einschliefen.
sentences.l: In the evening they came into a great forest and were so tired from grief, hunger and the long way that they set themselves into a hollow tree and fell asleep.
sentences.i: Toward evening they came to a great forest, and were so weary from grief, hunger and the long road that they sat down in a hollow tree and fell asleep.
sentences.n: The “so … daß …” frame is result: ‘so tired that …’ “einschliefen” is the separable verb einschlafen (‘to fall asleep’), here fused in the subordinate clause.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen, als sie aufwachten, stand die Sonne schon hoch am Himmel und schien heiß in den Baum hinein.
sentences.l: On the next morning, when they awoke, the sun stood already high in the sky and shone hot into the tree.
sentences.i: The next morning, when they woke, the sun already stood high in the sky and shone hot into the tree.
sentences.n: “am andern Morgen” = the next morning (andern is the old spelling of anderen). “aufwachten” (aufwachen, to wake up) and “schien … hinein” (hineinscheinen, to shine in) are both separable.

sentences.t: Da sprach das Brüderchen „Schwesterchen, mich dürstet, wenn ich ein Brünnlein wüßte, ich gieng und tränk einmal; ich mein, ich hört eins rauschen.“
sentences.l: Then the little-brother spoke, “little-sister, I thirst; if I knew of a little-spring, I would go and drink for once; I think I heard one rushing.”
sentences.i: Then the little brother said, “Sister, I am thirsty; if I knew of a little brook, I would go and drink; I think I hear one rippling.”
sentences.n: “mich dürstet” is the old impersonal construction ‘it thirsts me’ = ‘I am thirsty’ (accusative mich). “gieng … tränk” are subjunctive II (‘I would go and drink’), tränk being the old form for tränke (from trinken). “Brünnlein” is the diminutive of Brunnen (‘little spring, brooklet’), central to this tale. “im Rauschen” / “rauschen” is the murmuring rush of the water — you will meet it again as the brooks speak.

sentences.t: Brüderchen stand auf, nahm Schwesterchen an der Hand, und sie wollten das Brünnlein suchen.
sentences.l: Little-brother stood up, took little-sister by the hand, and they wanted to seek the little-spring.
sentences.i: Little brother got up, took little sister by the hand, and they set out to find the brook.
sentences.n: “stand auf” is the separable verb aufstehen (‘to get up’).

sentences.t: Die böse Stiefmutter aber war eine Hexe und hatte wohl gesehen wie die beiden Kinder fortgegangen waren, war ihnen nachgeschlichen, heimlich, wie die Hexen schleichen, und hatte alle Brunnen im Walde verwünscht.
sentences.l: But the wicked stepmother was a witch and had indeed seen how the two children had gone away, had crept after them, secretly, as witches creep, and had put a curse on all the springs in the forest.
sentences.i: But the wicked stepmother was a witch, and she had seen well enough how the two children had gone off; she had crept after them, secretly, the way witches creep, and had cursed every spring in the forest.
sentences.n: “nachgeschlichen” is the past participle of the separable nachschleichen (‘to creep after’); the plain verb “schleichen” (to creep, sneak) follows in the same breath. “im Walde” keeps the old dative -e on Wald. This curse is what makes the brooks speak.

sentences.t: Als sie nun ein Brünnlein fanden, das so glitzerig über die Steine sprang, wollte das Brüderchen daraus trinken: aber das Schwesterchen hörte wie es im Rauschen sprach „wer aus mir trinkt, wird ein Tiger: wer aus mir trinkt, wird ein Tiger.“
sentences.l: When they now found a little-spring that leapt so glitteringly over the stones, the little-brother wanted to drink from it: but the little-sister heard how it spoke in its rushing, “whoever drinks from me becomes a tiger: whoever drinks from me becomes a tiger.”
sentences.i: Now when they found a little brook that leapt so sparkling over the stones, the little brother wanted to drink from it; but the little sister heard how it spoke in its rushing: “Whoever drinks from me becomes a tiger; whoever drinks from me becomes a tiger.”
sentences.n: The first of the three brook-warnings. Note the doubled refrain and its fixed shape — “wer aus mir trinkt, wird ein …” (‘whoever drinks from me becomes a …’) — repeated at each spring with the beast changed (Tiger, then Wolf, then Reh). The wer-clause is a headless relative, ‘whoever’; the main verb wird (‘becomes’) follows it. “glitzerig” = glittering, sparkling.

sentences.t: Da rief das Schwesterchen „ich bitte dich, Brüderchen, trink nicht, sonst wirst du ein wildes Thier und zerreißest mich.“
sentences.l: Then the little-sister cried, “I beg you, little-brother, drink not, else you become a wild beast and tear me apart.”
sentences.i: Then the little sister cried, “I beg you, brother, do not drink, or you will become a wild beast and tear me to pieces.”
sentences.n: “Thier” is the 1857 spelling of Tier (beast), with the old -th- (so too Thür, thun). “zerreißest” is the 2nd-person of zerreißen (‘to tear apart’); the prefix zer- means ‘to pieces.’ “sonst” = else, otherwise.

sentences.t: Das Brüderchen trank nicht, ob es gleich so großen Durst hatte, und sprach „ich will warten bis zur nächsten Quelle.“
sentences.l: The little-brother did not drink, although he had such great thirst, and spoke, “I will wait until the next spring.”
sentences.i: The little brother did not drink, though his thirst was so great, and said, “I will wait until the next spring.”
sentences.n: “ob … gleich” is an old concessive, ‘although, even though’ (modern obgleich / obwohl). “es” refers to Brüderchen as das Kind (neuter), grammatically ‘it’ — read as ‘he.’

sentences.t: Als sie zum zweiten Brünnlein kamen, hörte das Schwesterchen wie auch dieses sprach „wer aus mir trinkt, wird ein Wolf: wer aus mir trinkt, wird ein Wolf.“
sentences.l: When they came to the second little-spring, the little-sister heard how this one too spoke, “whoever drinks from me becomes a wolf: whoever drinks from me becomes a wolf.”
sentences.i: When they came to the second brook, the little sister heard how this one too spoke: “Whoever drinks from me becomes a wolf; whoever drinks from me becomes a wolf.”
sentences.n: The refrain returns with the beast changed to a wolf — the middle of the three warnings.

sentences.t: Da rief das Schwesterchen „Brüderchen, ich bitte dich, trink nicht, sonst wirst du ein Wolf und frissest mich.“
sentences.l: Then the little-sister cried, “little-brother, I beg you, drink not, else you become a wolf and devour me.”
sentences.i: Then the little sister cried, “Brother, I beg you, do not drink, or you will become a wolf and devour me.”
sentences.n: “frissest” is the 2nd-person of fressen — the verb for an animal eating, carrying a devouring, greedy force that essen (used of people) does not.

sentences.t: Das Brüderchen trank nicht und sprach „ich will warten, bis wir zur nächsten Quelle kommen, aber dann muß ich trinken, du magst sagen, was du willst: mein Durst ist gar zu groß.“
sentences.l: The little-brother did not drink and spoke, “I will wait until we come to the next spring, but then I must drink, you may say what you will: my thirst is far too great.”
sentences.i: The little brother did not drink and said, “I will wait until we come to the next spring, but then I must drink, say what you like: my thirst is far too great.”
sentences.n: “muß” is the 1857 spelling of muss (‘must’), the ß kept throughout. “du magst sagen, was du willst” — mögen here in its old permissive sense, ‘you may say whatever you like.’ “gar zu groß” = far too great.

sentences.t: Und als sie zum dritten Brünnlein kamen, hörte das Schwesterlein, wie es im Rauschen sprach „wer aus mir trinkt, wird ein Reh: wer aus mir trinkt, wird ein Reh.“
sentences.l: And when they came to the third little-spring, the little-sister heard how it spoke in its rushing, “whoever drinks from me becomes a roe: whoever drinks from me becomes a roe.”
sentences.i: And when they came to the third brook, the little sister heard how it spoke in its rushing: “Whoever drinks from me becomes a roe; whoever drinks from me becomes a roe.”
sentences.n: The third and fatal warning: the beast is now a Reh (roe deer), the gentle creature the brother is about to become. “Schwesterlein” is a second diminutive of Schwester (with -lein rather than -chen), used interchangeably with Schwesterchen.

sentences.t: Das Schwesterchen sprach „ach Brüderchen, ich bitte dich, trink nicht, sonst wirst du ein Reh und läufst mir fort.“
sentences.l: The little-sister spoke, “ah little-brother, I beg you, drink not, else you become a roe and run away from me.”
sentences.i: The little sister said, “Oh brother, I beg you, do not drink, or you will become a roe and run away from me.”
sentences.n: “läufst … fort” is the separable verb fortlaufen (‘to run away’); the dative “mir” is a dative of interest, ‘run off on me.’

sentences.t: Aber das Brüderchen hatte sich gleich beim Brünnlein nieder geknieet, hinab gebeugt und von dem Wasser getrunken, und wie die ersten Tropfen auf seine Lippen gekommen waren, lag es da als ein Rehkälbchen.
sentences.l: But the little-brother had at once knelt down by the little-spring, bent down and drunk of the water, and as the first drops had come upon his lips, there it lay as a little fawn.
sentences.i: But the little brother had at once knelt down by the brook, bent down and drunk of the water, and as the first drops touched his lips, there he lay, a little fawn.
sentences.n: The transformation itself — the third drink, the warning ignored. “geknieet” is the 1857 spelling of gekniet (knelt, from knien); “nieder geknieet” and “hinab gebeugt” pair a prefix of downward motion with each participle. “Rehkälbchen” is a double diminutive, ‘little roe-calf, little fawn’ — the shape the brother keeps for most of the tale (also called Rehchen, Rehlein).

sentences.t: Nun weinte das Schwesterchen über das arme verwünschte Brüderchen, und das Rehchen weinte auch und saß so traurig neben ihm.
sentences.l: Now the little-sister wept over the poor enchanted little-brother, and the little-roe wept too and sat so sadly beside him.
sentences.i: Now the little sister wept over her poor bewitched brother, and the little roe wept too, and sat so sorrowfully beside her.
sentences.n: “verwünscht” = cursed, put under a spell (from verwünschen, which named the witch’s deed above). “Rehchen” is the little-roe diminutive that now names the brother.

sentences.t: Da sprach das Mädchen endlich „sei still, liebes Rehchen, ich will dich ja nimmermehr verlassen.“
sentences.l: Then the maiden spoke at last, “be still, dear little-roe, I will surely nevermore forsake you.”
sentences.i: Then the girl said at last, “Be still, dear little roe, I will never, never forsake you.”
sentences.n: “ja” here is an emphasizing particle, ‘surely, indeed.’ “nimmermehr” = nevermore (an emphatic, elevated ‘never again’).

sentences.t: Dann band es sein goldenes Strumpfband ab und that es dem Rehchen um den Hals, und rupfte Binsen und flocht ein weiches Seil daraus.
sentences.l: Then it untied its golden garter and put it around the little-roe’s neck, and plucked rushes and plaited a soft rope out of them.
sentences.i: Then she took off her golden garter and put it round the little roe’s neck, and plucked rushes and plaited a soft rope out of them.
sentences.n: “band … ab” is the separable verb abbinden (‘to untie, take off’). “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (‘did, put,’ from thun = modern tun). “Binsen” are rushes (reeds); the “Binsenseil” (rush-rope) she plaits will lead the roe on. “es” = the girl as das Mädchen (neuter), so grammatically ‘it.’

sentences.t: Daran band es das Thierchen und führte es weiter, und gieng immer tiefer in den Wald hinein.
sentences.l: To it it tied the little-beast and led it onward, and went ever deeper into the forest.
sentences.i: To this she tied the little creature and led it on, going ever deeper into the forest.
sentences.n: “Thierchen” is the diminutive of Thier (Tier), ‘little creature.’ “gieng … hinein” pairs the old gieng with hinein (‘in, away from the speaker’). “immer tiefer” = ever deeper.

sentences.t: Und als sie lange lange gegangen waren, kamen sie endlich an ein kleines Haus, und das Mädchen schaute hinein, und weil es leer war, dachte es „hier können wir bleiben und wohnen.“
sentences.l: And when they had walked long, long, they came at last to a little house, and the maiden looked in, and because it was empty, it thought, “here we can stay and dwell.”
sentences.i: And when they had walked on and on, they came at last to a little house, and the girl looked inside, and because it was empty, she thought, “Here we can stay and live.”
sentences.n: The repeated “lange lange” intensifies by doubling, a folk-narrative habit. “schaute hinein” (hineinschauen, to look in) is separable.

sentences.t: Da suchte es dem Rehchen Laub und Moos zu einem weichen Lager, und jeden Morgen gieng es aus und sammelte sich Wurzeln, Beeren und Nüsse, und für das Rehchen brachte es zartes Gras mit, das fraß es ihm aus der Hand, war vergnügt und spielte vor ihm herum.
sentences.l: There it sought for the little-roe leaves and moss for a soft bed, and every morning it went out and gathered itself roots, berries and nuts, and for the little-roe it brought tender grass along, that it ate out of its hand, was merry and played about before it.
sentences.i: There she gathered leaves and moss for the little roe’s soft bed, and every morning she went out and gathered roots, berries and nuts for herself, and brought tender grass for the little roe, which ate it from her hand, was happy and frolicked about before her.
sentences.n: A string of clauses sharing the same subject “es” (the girl). “suchte … dem Rehchen” uses the dative-of-benefit, ‘gathered for the little roe.’ “gieng … aus” (ausgehen) and “brachte … mit” (mitbringen) are separable. “Lager” here = a bed, resting-place, couch. The fronted “das” resumes the grass: ‘that it ate from her hand.’

sentences.t: Abends wenn Schwesterchen müde war und sein Gebet gesagt hatte, legte es seinen Kopf auf den Rücken des Rehkälbchens, das war sein Kissen, darauf es sanft einschlief.
sentences.l: In the evening, when little-sister was tired and had said her prayer, it laid its head on the back of the little-fawn, that was its pillow, upon which it softly fell asleep.
sentences.i: In the evening, when little sister was tired and had said her prayer, she laid her head on the little fawn’s back — that was her pillow — and on it she gently fell asleep.
sentences.n: “sein Gebet” — sein is the neuter possessive agreeing with das Schwesterchen (neuter), so grammatically ‘its,’ meaning ‘her.’ “des Rehkälbchens” is genitive (‘of the little fawn’). “darauf … einschlief” — darauf ‘upon which’; einschlafen is separable but stays together in the relative clause.

sentences.t: Und hätte das Brüderchen nur seine menschliche Gestalt gehabt, es wäre ein herrliches Leben gewesen.
sentences.l: And had the little-brother only had his human shape, it would have been a glorious life.
sentences.i: And had the little brother only kept his human shape, it would have been a glorious life.
sentences.n: A conditional with the verb fronted (no wenn): “hätte … gehabt … es wäre … gewesen” — subjunctive II throughout, the contrary-to-fact ‘had he only …, it would have been …’ “Gestalt” = shape, form (the human form he has lost).

sentences.t: Das dauerte eine Zeitlang, daß sie so allein in der Wildnis waren.
sentences.l: That lasted a while, that they were so alone in the wilderness.
sentences.i: So it went for a while, that they lived alone like this in the wilderness.
sentences.n: “eine Zeitlang” = for a while, a stretch of time. “Wildnis” = wilderness.

sentences.t: Es trug sich aber zu, daß der König des Landes eine große Jagd in dem Wald hielt.
sentences.l: But it came about that the king of the land held a great hunt in the forest.
sentences.i: But it came to pass that the king of the land held a great hunt in the forest.
sentences.n: “trug sich … zu” is the separable reflexive sich zutragen (‘to come about, happen’), a solemn fairy-tale phrase (compare English ‘it came to pass’). “des Landes” is genitive, ‘of the land.’

sentences.t: Da schallte das Hörnerblasen, Hundegebell und das lustige Geschrei der Jäger durch die Bäume, und das Rehlein hörte es und wäre gar zu gerne dabei gewesen.
sentences.l: Then the blowing-of-horns, barking-of-dogs and the merry shouting of the hunters resounded through the trees, and the little-roe heard it and would far too gladly have been there.
sentences.i: The blowing of horns, the baying of the hounds and the merry shouts of the huntsmen rang through the trees, and the little roe heard it and would so dearly have liked to be there.
sentences.n: German freely builds such compounds: “Hörnerblasen” (horn-blowing), “Hundegebell” (dog-barking). “wäre … gewesen” is subjunctive II (‘would have been’); “gar zu gerne” = ever so gladly. This longing for the hunt drives the rest of the tale.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sprach es zum Schwesterlein, „laß mich hinaus in die Jagd, ich kanns nicht länger mehr aushalten,“ und bat so lange, bis es einwilligte.
sentences.l: “Ah,” it spoke to the little-sister, “let me out into the hunt, I can bear it no longer,” and begged so long until she consented.
sentences.i: “Oh,” it said to the little sister, “let me out to the hunt, I can bear it no longer!” — and begged and begged until she gave in.
sentences.n: “kanns” = kann es (‘can [bear] it’), a contraction. “aushalten” = to endure, hold out. “bat so lange, bis …” = begged until — the ‘so long until’ construction for wearing someone down. “einwilligte” (einwilligen, to consent) is separable.

sentences.t: „Aber,“ sprach es zu ihm, „komm mir ja Abends wieder, vor den wilden Jägern schließ ich mein Thürlein; und damit ich dich kenne, so klopf und sprich mein Schwesterlein, laß mich herein: und wenn du nicht so sprichst, so schließ ich mein Thürlein nicht auf.“
sentences.l: “But,” she spoke to it, “do come back to me in the evening; against the wild hunters I shut my little-door; and so that I know you, knock and say ‘my little-sister, let me in’: and if you do not speak so, then I do not unlock my little-door.”
sentences.i: “But,” she said to it, “be sure to come back to me in the evening; I lock my little door against the wild huntsmen; and so that I know you, knock and say, ‘My little sister, let me in’; and if you do not speak so, I will not unlock my little door.”
sentences.n: “komm mir ja … wieder” — ja here strengthens the command, ‘be sure to.’ “Thürlein” is the diminutive of Thür (Tür), ‘little door.’ “klopf,” “sprich” are imperatives (klopfen, sprechen). This knock-and-password is the tale’s recurring pass-phrase; “schließ … auf” is the separable aufschließen (‘to unlock’).

sentences.t: Nun sprang das Rehchen hinaus, und war ihm so wohl und war so lustig in freier Luft.
sentences.l: Now the little-roe sprang out, and it was so well to it and it was so merry in the free air.
sentences.i: Now the little roe sprang out, and it felt so well and so merry in the open air.
sentences.n: “war ihm so wohl” is the impersonal dative idiom es ist jemandem wohl, ‘it is well with someone, someone feels good.’ “sprang … hinaus” (hinausspringen) is separable.

sentences.t: Der König und seine Jäger sahen das schöne Thier und setzten ihm nach, aber sie konnten es nicht einholen, und wenn sie meinten, sie hätten es gewiß, da sprang es über das Gebüsch weg und war verschwunden.
sentences.l: The king and his hunters saw the beautiful beast and set after it, but they could not catch it up, and whenever they thought they had it for sure, then it sprang away over the bushes and was vanished.
sentences.i: The king and his huntsmen saw the beautiful creature and gave chase, but they could not overtake it, and whenever they thought they had it for certain, it leapt away over the bushes and was gone.
sentences.n: “setzten … nach” (nachsetzen, to give chase) and “sprang … weg” (wegspringen) are separable. “einholen” = to catch up with, overtake. “gewiß” (1857 spelling of gewiss) = for certain; “hätten … gewiß” is subjunctive of their supposing.

sentences.t: Als es dunkel ward, lief es zu dem Häuschen, klopfte und sprach „mein Schwesterlein, laß mich herein.“
sentences.l: When it grew dark, it ran to the little house, knocked and spoke, “my little-sister, let me in.”
sentences.i: When it grew dark, it ran to the little house, knocked, and said, “My little sister, let me in.”
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became, grew’); modern German uses wurde. Here the roe gives the agreed password for the first time.

sentences.t: Da ward ihm die kleine Thür aufgethan, es sprang hinein und ruhete sich die ganze Nacht auf seinem weichen Lager aus.
sentences.l: Then the little door was opened to it, it sprang in and rested itself out the whole night on its soft bed.
sentences.i: Then the little door was opened to it, it sprang in and rested the whole night on its soft bed.
sentences.n: “ward … aufgethan” — ward forms the passive (‘was opened’); “aufgethan” is the 1857 spelling of aufgetan, the participle of the separable aufthun (to open). “ruhete … aus” is the separable ausruhen (to rest); ruhete is the old spelling of ruhte.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen gieng die Jagd von neuem an, und als das Rehlein wieder das Hüfthorn hörte und das ho, ho! der Jäger, da hatte es keine Ruhe, und sprach „Schwesterchen, mach mir auf, ich muß hinaus.“
sentences.l: On the next morning the hunt started up anew, and when the little-roe again heard the hip-horn and the ‘ho, ho!’ of the hunters, then it had no peace, and spoke, “little-sister, open up for me, I must out.”
sentences.i: The next morning the hunt began again, and when the little roe again heard the hunting-horn and the ‘ho, ho!’ of the huntsmen, it had no peace, and said, “Sister, open the door for me, I must go out.”
sentences.n: “gieng … an” is the separable angehen (‘to begin, start up’); “von neuem” = anew. “Hüfthorn” is a hunting horn carried at the hip. “mach … auf” (aufmachen) is separable; “ich muß hinaus” drops the verb of motion after the modal (‘I must [go] out’).

sentences.t: Das Schwesterchen öffnete ihm die Thüre und sprach „aber zu Abend mußt du wieder da sein und dein Sprüchlein sagen.“
sentences.l: The little-sister opened the door for it and spoke, “but by evening you must be here again and say your little-saying.”
sentences.i: The little sister opened the door for it and said, “But by evening you must be back again and say your little verse.”
sentences.n: “zu Abend” = toward/by evening. “Sprüchlein” is the diminutive of Spruch, ‘little saying, little verse’ — the password. “öffnete ihm die Thüre” uses the dative ihm, ‘opened the door for it.’

sentences.t: Als der König und seine Jäger das Rehlein mit dem goldenen Halsband wieder sahen, jagten sie ihm alle nach, aber es war ihnen zu schnell und behend.
sentences.l: When the king and his hunters saw the little-roe with the golden collar again, they all chased after it, but it was too fast and nimble for them.
sentences.i: When the king and his huntsmen saw the little roe with the golden collar again, they all gave chase, but it was too swift and nimble for them.
sentences.n: “jagten … nach” is the separable nachjagen (‘to chase after’). The golden garter round its neck now reads as a “Halsband” (collar) — the mark by which the king will know it. “behend” = nimble, agile.

sentences.t: Das währte den ganzen Tag, endlich aber hatten es die Jäger Abends umzingelt, und einer verwundete es ein wenig am Fuß, so daß es hinken mußte und langsam fortlief.
sentences.l: That lasted the whole day, but at last the hunters had surrounded it by evening, and one wounded it a little on the foot, so that it had to limp and ran off slowly.
sentences.i: This went on the whole day, but at last, toward evening, the huntsmen had it surrounded, and one of them wounded it a little in the foot, so that it had to limp and ran off slowly.
sentences.n: “währte” is the old preterite of währen (‘to last, endure’). “umzingelt” (umzingeln, to encircle) here has no ge- prefix in the participle, as verbs stressed on the second syllable do. “hinken” = to limp; “fortlief” (fortlaufen) is separable.

sentences.t: Da schlich ihm ein Jäger nach bis zu dem Häuschen und hörte wie es rief „mein Schwesterlein, laß mich herein,“ und sah daß die Thür ihm aufgethan und alsbald wieder zugeschlossen ward.
sentences.l: Then a hunter crept after it up to the little house and heard how it called, “my little-sister, let me in,” and saw that the door was opened to it and at once shut again.
sentences.i: Then a huntsman crept after it up to the little house and heard how it called, “My little sister, let me in,” and saw that the door was opened to it and at once shut again.
sentences.n: “schlich … nach” is the separable nachschleichen (‘to creep after’). “alsbald” = at once, presently (elevated). “zugeschlossen ward” — the passive with archaic ward; zuschließen (to shut, lock up) is separable.

sentences.t: Der Jäger behielt das alles wohl im Sinn, gieng zum König und erzählte ihm was er gesehen und gehört hatte.
sentences.l: The hunter kept all this well in mind, went to the king and told him what he had seen and heard.
sentences.i: The huntsman kept it all well in mind, went to the king and told him what he had seen and heard.
sentences.n: “im Sinn behalten” = to keep in mind, remember.

sentences.t: Da sprach der König „morgen soll noch einmal gejagt werden.“
sentences.l: Then the king spoke, “tomorrow there shall be hunting once more.”
sentences.i: Then the king said, “Tomorrow there shall be one more hunt.”
sentences.n: “soll … gejagt werden” is an impersonal passive, literally ‘it shall be hunted,’ i.e. ‘there shall be a hunt.’ “noch einmal” = once more.

sentences.t: Das Schwesterchen aber erschrack gewaltig, als es sah daß sein Rehkälbchen verwundet war.
sentences.l: But the little-sister took fright mightily when it saw that its little-fawn was wounded.
sentences.i: But the little sister was terribly frightened when she saw that her little fawn was wounded.
sentences.n: “erschrack” is the 1857/archaic preterite of intransitive erschrecken (‘took fright’); modern spelling erschrak. “gewaltig” here = mightily, terribly.

sentences.t: Es wusch ihm das Blut ab, legte Kräuter auf und sprach „geh auf dein Lager, lieb Rehchen, daß du wieder heil wirst.“
sentences.l: It washed the blood off it, laid herbs on and spoke, “go onto your bed, dear little-roe, that you become whole again.”
sentences.i: She washed the blood off it, laid herbs on the wound, and said, “Go to your bed, dear little roe, so that you may heal again.”
sentences.n: “wusch … ab” (abwaschen) and “legte … auf” (auflegen) are separable. “heil” = whole, healed, sound. The daß-clause here is one of purpose, ‘so that.’

sentences.t: Die Wunde aber war so gering, daß das Rehchen am Morgen nichts mehr davon spürte.
sentences.l: But the wound was so slight that the little-roe felt nothing more of it in the morning.
sentences.i: But the wound was so slight that by morning the little roe felt nothing more of it.
sentences.n: “gering” = slight, small. “nichts mehr davon” = nothing more of it.

sentences.t: Und als es die Jagdlust wieder draußen hörte, sprach es „ich kanns nicht aushalten, ich muß dabei sein; so bald soll mich keiner kriegen.“
sentences.l: And when it again heard the hunting-joy outside, it spoke, “I can’t bear it, I must be there; so soon shall no one catch me.”
sentences.i: And when it again heard the hunt astir outside, it said, “I cannot bear it, I must be there; no one will catch me so easily.”
sentences.n: “Jagdlust” = the joy/zest of the hunt. “so bald soll mich keiner kriegen” = ‘no one will get me that quickly’; kriegen is colloquial for ‘to catch, get.’

sentences.t: Das Schwesterchen weinte und sprach „nun werden sie dich tödten, und ich bin hier allein im Wald und bin verlassen von aller Welt: ich laß dich nicht hinaus.“
sentences.l: The little-sister wept and spoke, “now they will kill you, and I am here alone in the forest and am forsaken by all the world: I do not let you out.”
sentences.i: The little sister wept and said, “Now they will kill you, and I am here alone in the forest, forsaken by all the world: I will not let you out.”
sentences.n: “tödten” is the 1857 spelling of töten (‘to kill’), with the old -dt-. “von aller Welt verlassen” = forsaken by everyone.

sentences.t: „So sterb ich dir hier vor Betrübnis,“ antwortete das Rehchen, „wenn ich das Hüfthorn höre, so mein ich, ich müßt aus den Schuhen springen!“
sentences.l: “Then I die here on you of grief,” answered the little-roe, “when I hear the hip-horn, then I think I would have to spring out of my shoes!”
sentences.i: “Then I shall die here of grief, on your hands,” answered the little roe, “when I hear the hunting-horn, I feel as if I must leap out of my very skin!”
sentences.n: “sterb ich dir” — the dative dir is a dative of interest, ‘I’ll die on you.’ “Betrübnis” = grief, sorrow. “müßt” is subjunctive II of müssen (‘would have to’); “aus den Schuhen springen” is a folk exaggeration for bursting with excitement (literally ‘to leap out of one’s shoes’).

sentences.t: Da konnte das Schwesterchen nicht anders und schloß ihm mit schwerem Herzen die Thür auf, und das Rehchen sprang gesund und fröhlich in den Wald.
sentences.l: Then the little-sister could not do otherwise and unlocked the door for it with a heavy heart, and the little-roe sprang healthy and merry into the forest.
sentences.i: Then the little sister could do no other, and with a heavy heart unlocked the door for it, and the little roe sprang, hale and merry, into the forest.
sentences.n: “konnte nicht anders” = could not do otherwise. “schloß … auf” is the separable aufschließen (to unlock); schloß is the 1857 spelling of schloss. “mit schwerem Herzen” = with a heavy heart.

sentences.t: Als es der König erblickte, sprach er zu seinen Jägern „nun jagt ihm nach den ganzen Tag bis in die Nacht, aber daß ihm keiner etwas zu Leide thut.“
sentences.l: When the king caught sight of it, he spoke to his hunters, “now chase after it the whole day into the night, but that none does it any harm.”
sentences.i: When the king caught sight of it, he said to his huntsmen, “Now chase after it the whole day, on into the night, but see that none of you does it any harm.”
sentences.n: “erblicken” = to catch sight of, behold. “jagt … nach” is the plural imperative of nachjagen. “einem etwas zu Leide thun” is a fixed idiom, ‘to do someone harm’ (Leide, dative of Leid; thut is the 1857 spelling of tut). The bare “aber daß …” carries an imperative force, ‘but see that …’

sentences.t: Sobald die Sonne untergegangen war, sprach der König zum Jäger „nun komm und zeige mir das Waldhäuschen.“
sentences.l: As soon as the sun had gone down, the king spoke to the hunter, “now come and show me the little forest-house.”
sentences.i: As soon as the sun had gone down, the king said to the huntsman, “Now come and show me the little forest house.”
sentences.n: “untergegangen” is the past participle of the separable untergehen (‘to go down, set’). “zeige” is the imperative of zeigen. “Waldhäuschen” = little forest house (Wald + Häuschen).

sentences.t: Und als er vor dem Thürlein war, klopfte er an und rief „lieb Schwesterlein, laß mich herein.“
sentences.l: And when he was before the little-door, he knocked and called, “dear little-sister, let me in.”
sentences.i: And when he stood before the little door, he knocked and called, “Dear little sister, let me in.”
sentences.n: “klopfte … an” is the separable anklopfen (‘to knock’). The king borrows the roe’s password to gain entry.

sentences.t: Da gieng die Thür auf, und der König trat herein, und da stand ein Mädchen, das war so schön wie er noch keins gesehen hatte.
sentences.l: Then the door opened, and the king stepped in, and there stood a maiden, who was so beautiful as he had yet seen none.
sentences.i: Then the door opened, and the king stepped in, and there stood a maiden as beautiful as any he had ever seen.
sentences.n: “gieng … auf” is the separable aufgehen (‘to open, go open’), old spelling for aufging. “so schön wie er noch keins gesehen hatte” = literally ‘as beautiful as he had seen none yet,’ i.e. more beautiful than any he had seen.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen erschrack als es sah daß nicht sein Rehlein sondern ein Mann herein kam, der eine goldene Krone auf dem Haupt hatte.
sentences.l: The maiden took fright when it saw that not its little-roe but a man came in, who had a golden crown on his head.
sentences.i: The maiden was startled when she saw that it was not her little roe but a man who came in, with a golden crown on his head.
sentences.n: “nicht … sondern” = ‘not … but (rather).’ “kam … herein” (hereinkommen) is separable. “Haupt” is an elevated word for the head (modern Kopf).

sentences.t: Aber der König sah es freundlich an, reichte ihm die Hand und sprach „willst du mit mir gehen auf mein Schloß und meine liebe Frau sein?“
sentences.l: But the king looked at it kindly, reached out his hand to it and spoke, “will you go with me to my castle and be my dear wife?”
sentences.i: But the king looked at her kindly, held out his hand to her, and said, “Will you go with me to my castle and be my dear wife?”
sentences.n: “sah … an” is the separable ansehen (‘to look at’). “reichte ihm die Hand” = held out his hand to her (dative ihm). The neuter es/ihm still refers to das Mädchen.

sentences.t: „Ach ja,“ antwortete das Mädchen, „aber das Rehchen muß auch mit, das verlaß ich nicht.“
sentences.l: “Oh yes,” answered the maiden, “but the little-roe must come too, that I do not forsake.”
sentences.i: “Oh yes,” answered the maiden, “but the little roe must come too — I will not leave it behind.”
sentences.n: “muß auch mit” drops the verb of motion after the modal (‘must [come] along too’). “verlaß” is the 1st-person present of verlassen (‘to forsake, leave’); the fronted “das” resumes the roe for emphasis.

sentences.t: Sprach der König „es soll bei dir bleiben, so lange du lebst, und soll ihm an nichts fehlen.“
sentences.l: Spoke the king, “it shall stay with you as long as you live, and shall lack nothing.”
sentences.i: The king said, “It shall stay with you as long as you live, and shall want for nothing.”
sentences.n: “es soll … ihm an nichts fehlen” — the idiom jemandem fehlt es an nichts, ‘someone lacks nothing’ (impersonal, with dative ihm). “so lange du lebst” = as long as you live.

sentences.t: Indem kam es hereingesprungen, da band es das Schwesterchen wieder an das Binsenseil, nahm es selbst in die Hand und gieng mit ihm aus dem Waldhäuschen fort.
sentences.l: Just then it came leaping in; then the little-sister tied it again to the rush-rope, took it herself in her hand and went away with it out of the little forest-house.
sentences.i: Just then it came leaping in; so the little sister tied it again to the rush-rope, took the rope herself in her hand, and went off with it out of the little forest house.
sentences.n: “Indem” here = ‘just then, in that moment.’ “kam … hereingesprungen” = came leaping in (hereinspringen, past participle after kommen showing manner of arrival). “gieng … fort” (fortgehen) is separable. “Binsenseil” = the rush-rope she plaited earlier.

sentences.t: Der König nahm das schöne Mädchen auf sein Pferd und führte es in sein Schloß, wo die Hochzeit mit großer Pracht gefeiert wurde, und war es nun die Frau Königin, und lebten sie lange Zeit vergnügt zusammen; das Rehlein ward gehegt und gepflegt und sprang in dem Schloßgarten herum.
sentences.l: The king took the beautiful maiden onto his horse and led her to his castle, where the wedding was celebrated with great splendor, and now she was the lady queen, and they lived a long time contentedly together; the little-roe was tended and cared for and sprang about in the castle-garden.
sentences.i: The king took the beautiful maiden onto his horse and brought her to his castle, where the wedding was celebrated with great splendour, and now she was the queen, and they lived contentedly together a long while; the little roe was cherished and cared for, and roamed about the castle garden.
sentences.n: “gehegt und gepflegt” is a set alliterative pair, ‘cherished and cared for’ (hegen + pflegen). “ward” once more is the archaic passive/preterite of werden. “Schloßgarten” = castle garden; “sprang … herum” (herumspringen) is separable.

sentences.t: Die böse Stiefmutter aber, um derentwillen die Kinder in die Welt hineingegangen waren, die meinte nicht anders als Schwesterchen wäre von den wilden Thieren im Walde zerrissen worden und Brüderchen als ein Rehkalb von den Jägern todt geschossen.
sentences.l: But the wicked stepmother, on whose account the children had gone out into the world, she thought nothing other than that little-sister had been torn apart by the wild beasts in the forest and little-brother, as a roe-calf, shot dead by the hunters.
sentences.i: But the wicked stepmother, on whose account the children had gone out into the world, thought nothing other than that little sister had been torn to pieces by the wild beasts in the forest, and little brother, as a fawn, shot dead by the huntsmen.
sentences.n: “um derentwillen” = ‘on whose account, for whose sake’ (a relative built on the genitive plural). “meinte nicht anders als” = supposed nothing other than. The reported guesses stand in subjunctive: “wäre … zerrissen worden … [wäre] … geschossen.” “Thieren” (dative pl. of Thier) and “todt” keep the 1857 spellings.

sentences.t: Als sie nun hörte daß sie so glücklich waren, und es ihnen so wohl gieng, da wurden Neid und Mißgunst in ihrem Herzen rege und ließen ihr keine Ruhe, und sie hatte keinen andern Gedanken, als wie sie die beiden doch noch ins Unglück bringen könnte.
sentences.l: When she now heard that they were so happy, and it went so well with them, then envy and ill-will stirred in her heart and left her no peace, and she had no other thought than how she might yet bring the two of them into misfortune.
sentences.i: Now when she heard that they were so happy, and were faring so well, envy and ill-will stirred in her heart and gave her no peace, and she had no thought but how she might yet bring the two of them to ruin.
sentences.n: “es ihnen so wohl gieng” — the impersonal dative idiom jemandem geht es wohl, ‘to fare well.’ “Mißgunst” (1857 spelling) = ill-will, envious grudging. “wurden … rege” = grew astir, stirred (rege = stirring, active). “als wie … könnte” — könnte is subjunctive II of the possibility she plots.

sentences.t: Ihre rechte Tochter, die häßlich war wie die Nacht, und nur ein Auge hatte, die machte ihr Vorwürfe und sprach „eine Königin zu werden, das Glück hätte mir gebührt.“
sentences.l: Her own daughter, who was ugly as the night and had only one eye, she made reproaches to her and spoke, “to become a queen — that fortune ought to have been mine.”
sentences.i: Her own daughter, who was as ugly as night and had but one eye, reproached her and said, “To become a queen — that good fortune should have fallen to me.”
sentences.n: “rechte Tochter” here = her own true daughter. “Vorwürfe machen” = to reproach (jemandem, dative). “das Glück hätte mir gebührt” — gebühren means ‘to be due, befit’; in subjunctive II, ‘the fortune ought to have been due to me.’ The one eye becomes crucial later.

sentences.t: „Sei nur still,“ sagte die Alte und sprach sie zufrieden, „wenns Zeit ist, will ich schon bei der Hand sein.“
sentences.l: “Just be still,” said the old one and talked her content, “when it’s time, I will surely be at hand.”
sentences.i: “Only be still,” said the old woman, soothing her, “when the time comes, I shall be at hand, never fear.”
sentences.n: “die Alte” = the old woman (adjective used as noun), i.e. the witch. “sprach sie zufrieden” = talked her into contentment, soothed her. “wenns” = wenn es. “schon” here is reassuring, ‘surely, never fear’; “bei der Hand sein” = to be at hand, ready.

sentences.t: Als nun die Zeit heran gerückt war, und die Königin ein schönes Knäblein zur Welt gebracht hatte, und der König gerade auf der Jagd war, nahm die alte Hexe die Gestalt der Kammerfrau an, trat in die Stube, wo die Königin lag und sprach zu der Kranken „kommt, das Bad ist fertig, das wird euch wohlthun und frische Kräfte geben: geschwind, eh es kalt wird.“
sentences.l: When now the time had drawn near, and the queen had brought a fair little-boy into the world, and the king was just at the hunt, the old witch took on the shape of the chamber-woman, stepped into the room where the queen lay and spoke to the sick one, “come, the bath is ready, it will do you good and give fresh strength: quick, before it grows cold.”
sentences.i: Now when the time had come, and the queen had brought a fair little boy into the world, and the king was just then out hunting, the old witch took on the shape of the chambermaid, stepped into the room where the queen lay, and said to the invalid, “Come, the bath is ready; it will do you good and give you fresh strength — quickly, before it grows cold.”
sentences.n: “heran gerückt” — the separable heranrücken (‘to draw near’). “zur Welt bringen” = to give birth to. “Knäblein” = little boy (diminutive of Knabe). “die Gestalt … an … nahm” is the separable annehmen (‘to take on, assume’). “Kranken” = the sick one, invalid (dative of die Kranke). “wohlthun” (1857 for wohltun) = to do good, do one good.

sentences.t: Ihre Tochter war auch bei der Hand, sie trugen die schwache Königin in die Badstube und legten sie in die Wanne: dann schlossen sie die Thür ab und liefen davon.
sentences.l: Her daughter too was at hand; they carried the weak queen into the bath-chamber and laid her in the tub: then they locked the door and ran away.
sentences.i: Her daughter too was at hand; they carried the weak queen into the bath-chamber and laid her in the tub; then they locked the door and ran off.
sentences.n: “Badstube” = bath-chamber, bathhouse room; “Wanne” = tub, bathtub. “schlossen … ab” is the separable abschließen (‘to lock’). “liefen davon” = ran away (davonlaufen).

sentences.t: In der Badstube aber hatten sie ein rechtes Höllenfeuer angemacht, daß die schöne junge Königin bald ersticken mußte.
sentences.l: But in the bath-chamber they had made a real hellfire, so that the beautiful young queen soon had to suffocate.
sentences.i: But in the bath-chamber they had lit a very inferno, so that the beautiful young queen was soon bound to suffocate.
sentences.n: “ein rechtes Höllenfeuer” = a veritable hellfire (rechtes here = ‘proper, real’). “angemacht” is the participle of the separable anmachen (‘to light, kindle’). The daß-clause is one of result. “ersticken” = to suffocate, smother.

sentences.t: Als das vollbracht war, nahm die Alte ihre Tochter, setzte ihr eine Haube auf, und legte sie ins Bett an der Königin Stelle.
sentences.l: When that was accomplished, the old one took her daughter, set a cap on her, and laid her in the bed in the queen’s place.
sentences.i: When that was done, the old woman took her daughter, set a cap on her, and laid her in the bed in the queen’s place.
sentences.n: “vollbracht” is the participle of vollbringen (‘to accomplish, carry out’). “setzte … auf” is the separable aufsetzen (‘to put on,’ of a hat/cap). “an der Königin Stelle” = in the queen’s place (an … Stelle, with Königin an old uninflected genitive).

sentences.t: Sie gab ihr auch die Gestalt und das Ansehen der Königin, nur das verlorene Auge konnte sie ihr nicht wieder geben.
sentences.l: She also gave her the shape and the appearance of the queen, only the lost eye she could not give her again.
sentences.i: She also gave her the shape and the look of the queen; only the lost eye she could not give her back.
sentences.n: “Ansehen” here = appearance, look. “verlorene” is the adjective from verloren (‘lost,’ participle of verlieren) — the daughter’s missing eye, which the magic cannot restore.

sentences.t: Damit es aber der König nicht merkte, mußte sie sich auf die Seite legen, wo sie kein Auge hatte.
sentences.l: But so that the king would not notice it, she had to lay herself on the side where she had no eye.
sentences.i: But so that the king should not notice, she had to lie on the side where she had no eye.
sentences.n: “Damit” = so that (purpose); it sends its verb to the end (“merkte”). “sich … legen” = to lie down (reflexive).

sentences.t: Am Abend, als er heim kam und hörte daß ihm ein Söhnlein geboren war, freute er sich herzlich, und wollte ans Bett seiner lieben Frau gehen und sehen was sie machte.
sentences.l: In the evening, when he came home and heard that a little-son had been born to him, he rejoiced heartily, and wanted to go to the bed of his dear wife and see what she was doing.
sentences.i: In the evening, when he came home and heard that a little son had been born to him, he rejoiced with all his heart, and wanted to go to his dear wife’s bed and see how she fared.
sentences.n: “heim kam” (heimkommen) is separable. “Söhnlein” = little son (diminutive of Sohn). “freute sich herzlich” = rejoiced heartily (sich freuen, reflexive; herzlich, from the heart). “was sie machte” = ‘what she was doing / how she was.’

sentences.t: Da rief die Alte geschwind „bei Leibe, laßt die Vorhänge zu, die Königin darf noch nicht ins Licht sehen und muß Ruhe haben.“
sentences.l: Then the old one called quickly, “on your life, leave the curtains shut, the queen may not yet look into the light and must have rest.”
sentences.i: Then the old woman quickly cried, “On no account — keep the curtains drawn! The queen must not look into the light yet, and must have rest.”
sentences.n: “geschwind” = quickly. “bei Leibe” is an old emphatic phrase, ‘on your life, on no account.’ “laßt … zu” = leave shut (lassen + zu). “darf … nicht” = may/must not (permission).

sentences.t: Der König gieng zurück und wußte nicht daß eine falsche Königin im Bette lag.
sentences.l: The king went back and did not know that a false queen lay in the bed.
sentences.i: The king went back, and did not know that a false queen lay in the bed.
sentences.n: “gieng zurück” (zurückgehen) is separable. “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste (‘knew’). “im Bette” keeps the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Als es aber Mitternacht war und alles schlief, da sah die Kinderfrau, die in der Kinderstube neben der Wiege saß und allein noch wachte, wie die Thüre aufgieng, und die rechte Königin herein trat.
sentences.l: But when it was midnight and all slept, the nurse, who sat in the nursery beside the cradle and alone still kept watch, saw how the door opened, and the true queen stepped in.
sentences.i: But when it was midnight and all lay asleep, the nurse — who sat in the nursery beside the cradle and alone still kept watch — saw how the door opened, and the true queen stepped in.
sentences.n: “Kinderfrau” = nursemaid, nanny; “Kinderstube” = nursery; “Wiege” = cradle. “wachen” = to keep watch, stay awake. “aufgieng” = opened (aufgehen, old spelling). “rechte Königin” here = the true, rightful queen. This begins the ghostly-return sequence.

sentences.t: Sie nahm das Kind aus der Wiege, legte es in ihren Arm und gab ihm zu trinken.
sentences.l: She took the child out of the cradle, laid it in her arm and gave it to drink.
sentences.i: She took the child from the cradle, laid it in her arm, and gave it to drink.
sentences.n: “gab ihm zu trinken” = gave it (something) to drink, i.e. nursed it (dative ihm).

sentences.t: Dann schüttelte sie ihm sein Kißchen, legte es wieder hinein und deckte es mit dem Deckbettchen zu.
sentences.l: Then she shook up its little-cushion for it, laid it back in and covered it up with the little coverlet.
sentences.i: Then she plumped up its little pillow, laid it back in, and tucked it up with the little coverlet.
sentences.n: “schüttelte ihm sein Kißchen” uses the dative-of-possession, ‘shook its little cushion’ (Kißchen, 1857 spelling of Kisschen, from Kissen). “legte … hinein” and “deckte … zu” (zudecken, ‘to cover up, tuck in’) are separable. “Deckbettchen” = little coverlet, small quilt.

sentences.t: Sie vergaß aber auch das Rehchen nicht, gieng in die Ecke, wo es lag, und streichelte ihm über den Rücken.
sentences.l: But she did not forget the little-roe either, went into the corner where it lay, and stroked it over the back.
sentences.i: But she did not forget the little roe either; she went to the corner where it lay and stroked it along the back.
sentences.n: “vergaß” is the preterite of vergessen (‘to forget’). “streichelte ihm über den Rücken” — again the dative ihm with a body-part, ‘stroked it over the back’ (streicheln = to stroke, caress).

sentences.t: Darauf gieng sie ganz stillschweigend wieder zur Thüre hinaus, und die Kinderfrau fragte am andern Morgen die Wächter ob jemand während der Nacht ins Schloß gegangen wäre, aber sie antworteten „nein, wir haben niemand gesehen.“
sentences.l: Thereupon she went, quite silently, out through the door again, and the nurse asked the watchmen the next morning whether anyone had gone into the castle during the night, but they answered, “no, we have seen no one.”
sentences.i: Thereupon she went out through the door again, quite silently, and the next morning the nurse asked the watchmen whether anyone had come into the castle during the night, but they answered, “No, we have seen no one.”
sentences.n: “stillschweigend” = silently, without a word. “gieng … hinaus” (hinausgehen) is separable. “Wächter” = watchmen, guards. “ob … gegangen wäre” — subjunctive II in the indirect question.

sentences.t: So kam sie viele Nächte und sprach niemals ein Wort dabei; die Kinderfrau sah sie immer, aber sie getraute sich nicht jemand etwas davon zu sagen.
sentences.l: So she came many nights and never spoke a word thereby; the nurse always saw her, but she did not dare to say anything of it to anyone.
sentences.i: So she came many nights and never spoke a word; the nurse always saw her, but she did not dare say anything of it to anyone.
sentences.n: “Nächte” = nights (plural of Nacht). “niemals” = never. “sich getrauen” = to dare (reflexive); “getraute sich nicht … zu sagen” = did not dare to tell.

sentences.t: Als nun so eine Zeit verflossen war, da hub die Königin in der Nacht an zu reden und sprach
sentences.l: When now such a time had passed, the queen began at night to speak and spoke
sentences.i: Now when such a time had passed, the queen one night began to speak, and said:
sentences.n: “verflossen” is the participle of verfließen (‘to elapse, pass’). “hub … an” is the archaic preterite of anfangen/anheben (‘to begin’); modern German would say fing an. The sentence breaks off deliberately — the verse that follows completes it.

sentences.t:
„was macht mein Kind? was macht mein Reh?
Nun komm ich noch zweimal und dann nimmermehr.“
sentences.l:
“How is my child? how is my roe?
Now I come two more times and then nevermore.”
sentences.i:
“How fares my child? how fares my roe?
Now twice more I come, and then nevermore.”
sentences.n: The ghostly queen’s nightly verse — spoken three times over three nights, each time counting down: zweimal (twice more), then einmal (once more), then diesmal (this time). “was macht mein Kind?” is the idiom ‘how is my child doing?’ “nimmermehr” = nevermore. Keep the two lines and the rhyme (Reh / nimmermehr).

sentences.t: Die Kinderfrau antwortete ihr nicht, aber als sie wieder verschwunden war, gieng sie zum König und erzählte ihm alles.
sentences.l: The nurse did not answer her, but when she had vanished again, she went to the king and told him everything.
sentences.i: The nurse did not answer her, but when she had vanished again, she went to the king and told him everything.
sentences.n: “verschwunden” is the participle of verschwinden (‘to disappear, vanish’).

sentences.t: Sprach der König „Ach Gott, was ist das! ich will in der nächsten Nacht bei dem Kinde wachen.“
sentences.l: Spoke the king, “Ah God, what is this! I will keep watch by the child the next night.”
sentences.i: The king said, “Oh God, what is this! The next night I will keep watch by the child.”
sentences.n: “bei dem Kinde wachen” = to keep watch beside the child (wachen, to stay awake and watch); Kinde keeps the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Abends gieng er in die Kinderstube, aber um Mitternacht erschien die Königin wieder und sprach
sentences.l: In the evening he went into the nursery, but at midnight the queen appeared again and spoke
sentences.i: In the evening he went into the nursery, but at midnight the queen appeared again, and said:
sentences.n: “erschien” is the preterite of erscheinen (‘to appear’). Again the sentence runs straight into the verse.

sentences.t:
„was macht mein Kind? was macht mein Reh?
Nun komm ich noch einmal und dann nimmermehr.“
sentences.l:
“How is my child? how is my roe?
Now I come one more time and then nevermore.”
sentences.i:
“How fares my child? how fares my roe?
Now once more I come, and then nevermore.”
sentences.n: The second night: the count falls from ‘twice more’ to ‘once more’ (einmal). Only the number changes; the rhyme (Reh / nimmermehr) holds.

sentences.t: Und pflegte dann des Kindes, wie sie gewöhnlich that, ehe sie verschwand.
sentences.l: And then tended the child, as she customarily did, before she vanished.
sentences.i: And then she tended the child, as she was wont to do, before she vanished.
sentences.n: “pflegte des Kindes” — pflegen here takes the genitive (‘to tend, care for,’ an older government); des Kindes is genitive. “gewöhnlich” = customarily; “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (‘did’). “ehe” = before.

sentences.t: Der König getraute sich nicht sie anzureden, aber er wachte auch in der folgenden Nacht.
sentences.l: The king did not dare to address her, but he kept watch also the following night.
sentences.i: The king did not dare to speak to her, but he kept watch the following night as well.
sentences.n: “sich getrauen” = to dare (reflexive). “anzureden” is the zu-infinitive of the separable anreden (‘to address, speak to’) — the zu slots between prefix and stem.

sentences.t: Sie sprach abermals
sentences.l: She spoke once again
sentences.i: Once again she said:
sentences.n: “abermals” = once again, anew (elevated). The verse follows.

sentences.t:
„was macht mein Kind? was macht mein Reh?
Nun komm ich noch diesmal und dann nimmermehr.“
sentences.l:
“How is my child? how is my roe?
Now I come this time and then nevermore.”
sentences.i:
“How fares my child? how fares my roe?
Now this once I come, and then nevermore.”
sentences.n: The third and last night: ‘this time’ (diesmal) — the final visit before ‘nevermore.’ It is this last appearance that moves the king to act.

sentences.t: Da konnte sich der König nicht zurückhalten, sprang zu ihr und sprach „du kannst niemand anders sein, als meine liebe Frau.“
sentences.l: Then the king could not hold himself back, sprang to her and spoke, “you can be no one other than my dear wife.”
sentences.i: Then the king could not restrain himself, sprang up to her, and said, “You can be no one but my dear wife.”
sentences.n: “sich zurückhalten” = to hold oneself back, restrain oneself (separable reflexive). “niemand anders … als” = no one other than.

sentences.t: Da antwortete sie „ja, ich bin deine liebe Frau,“ und hatte in dem Augenblick durch Gottes Gnade das Leben wieder erhalten, war frisch, roth und gesund.
sentences.l: Then she answered, “yes, I am your dear wife,” and had in that moment, through God’s grace, received life again, was fresh, red and healthy.
sentences.i: Then she answered, “Yes, I am your dear wife,” and in that moment, through God’s grace, she was given back her life, and was fresh, rosy and well.
sentences.n: “durch Gottes Gnade” = through God’s grace (Gottes, genitive of Gott). “das Leben wieder erhalten” — erhalten here = to receive, obtain. “roth” is the 1857 spelling of rot (red), with the old -th- — of her restored, healthy colour.

sentences.t: Darauf erzählte sie dem König den Frevel, den die böse Hexe und ihre Tochter an ihr verübt hatten.
sentences.l: Thereupon she told the king the outrage that the wicked witch and her daughter had committed against her.
sentences.i: Thereupon she told the king of the outrage the wicked witch and her daughter had committed against her.
sentences.n: “Frevel” = an outrage, wicked deed, sacrilege. “an ihr verübt” — verüben (‘to commit, perpetrate’) a crime; “an jemandem” = against someone.

sentences.t: Der König ließ beide vor Gericht führen, und es ward ihnen das Urtheil gesprochen.
sentences.l: The king had both led before the court, and the judgment was pronounced upon them.
sentences.i: The king had them both brought before the court, and sentence was pronounced upon them.
sentences.n: “ließ … führen” = had (them) led (lassen + infinitive, the causative). “vor Gericht” = before the court. “das Urtheil sprechen” = to pronounce the sentence (Urtheil, 1857 for Urteil; gesprochen, participle of sprechen); “ward” forms the passive.

sentences.t: Die Tochter ward in Wald geführt, wo sie die wilden Thiere zerrissen, die Hexe aber ward ins Feuer gelegt und mußte jammervoll verbrennen.
sentences.l: The daughter was led into the forest, where the wild beasts tore her apart; but the witch was laid into the fire and had to burn wretchedly.
sentences.i: The daughter was led into the forest, where the wild beasts tore her to pieces; but the witch was cast into the fire and had to burn in agony.
sentences.n: Grimm does not soften the punishments. “zerrissen” is here the preterite of zerreißen (‘tore apart’). “jammervoll” = full of woe, pitifully, in agony. “verbrennen” = to burn up, be consumed by fire.

sentences.t: Und wie sie zu Asche verbrannt war, verwandelte sich das Rehkälbchen und erhielt seine menschliche Gestalt wieder; Schwesterchen und Brüderchen aber lebten glücklich zusammen bis an ihr Ende.
sentences.l: And when she had burned to ashes, the little-fawn transformed itself and received its human shape again; but little-sister and little-brother lived happily together until their end.
sentences.i: And when she had burned to ashes, the little fawn was transformed and received its human shape again; and little sister and little brother lived happily together to the end of their days.
sentences.n: The witch’s death breaks the spell. “verwandelte sich” — sich verwandeln, ‘to transform, be changed’ (reflexive). “erhielt … wieder” — erhalten, to receive; wieder, again. “bis an ihr Ende” = to the end of their days, the stock closing formula.
